perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall be disclosed i n written, electronic or other permanent form and shall be prominently identified as confidential using an appropriate legend, marking stamp, or other clear and conspicuous written identification which unambiguously indicates that the information being pro vided is the Disclosing Party 's Confidential Information. If Confidential Information is disclosed in other than written or other permanent form, it shall be considered Confidential Information as of the time of original disclosure if: i) the Disclosing Pa rty identifies it as Confidential Information at the time of the original disclosure and ii)
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall be disclosed i n written, electronic or other permanent form and may be identified as confidential using an appropriate legend, marking stamp, or other clear and conspicuous written identification which unambiguously indicates that the information being pro vided is the Disclosing Party 's Confidential Information. If Confidential Information is disclosed in other than written or other permanent form, it shall be considered Confidential Information as of the time of original disclosure if: i) the Disclosing Pa rty identifies it as Confidential Information at any time and ii)
perturbation.explanation: By changing "shall be prominently identified" to "may be identified" and "at the time of the original disclosure" to "at any time", we make the identification of confidential information optional and indefinite. This conflicts with typical NDA enforcement which requires reasonable steps to maintain confidentiality, including marking documents.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A - Regulation of Business Practices for Consumers Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, §2 pertains to unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. In the context of a non-disclosure agreement, a key aspect is ensuring that parties act fairly and in good faith to protect confidential information. By weakening the requirement for clear and timely identification of confidential information, the modified contract introduces ambiguity that could be construed as an unfair practice, potentially violating Chapter 93A.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by weakening the requirements for identifying confidential information. The original text mandates that confidential information 'shall be prominently identified,' while the changed text makes it optional by stating it 'may be identified.' Furthermore, the original text requires identification 'at the time of the original disclosure,' while the changed text allows identification 'at any time.' This conflicts with the intent of Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, §2, which addresses unfair practices in trade and commerce. While the snippets themselves don't explicitly state requirements for marking confidential information in an NDA, the Law Explanation correctly identifies that weakening these requirements could be construed as an unfair practice, violating Chapter 93A because it introduces ambiguity that could allow a party to later claim they didn't know information was confidential. The modification removes a core requirement for maintaining confidentiality, creating a scenario where the contract could fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall implement and maintain appropriate security measures for Personal Data which shall be at least as protective of the confidentiality of such information as the safeguards for personal information set forth under applicable federal, state and local law .
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall implement and maintain security measures for Personal Data which they deem appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to 'applicable federal, state and local law,' and stating the recipient will implement what they *deem* appropriate, we introduce a contradiction with data protection regulations. Many laws mandate specific security standards; this clause allows the Recipient to circumvent those standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93H - Security Breaches
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93H, §2 requires entities that own or license personal information of Massachusetts residents to develop, implement, and maintain a comprehensive information security program. This program must include administrative, technical, and physical safeguards to protect the personal information. By stating that the recipient shall implement security measures they *deem* appropriate, the modified clause allows the recipient to circumvent the specific requirements for data security outlined in M.G.L. c. 93H, thus contradicting state law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93H, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93H SECURITY BREACHES Section 2 Regulations to safeguard personal information of commonwealth residents Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Regulations to safeguard personal information of commonwealth residents Section 2. (a) The department of consumer affairs and business regulation shall adopt regulations relative to any person that owns or licenses personal information about a resident of the commonwealth. Such regulations shall be designed to safeguard the personal information of residents of the commonwealth and shall be consistent with the safeguards for protection of personal information set forth in the federal regulations by which the person is regulated. The objectives of the regulations shall be to: insure the security and confidentiality of customer information in a manner fully consistent with industry standards; protect against anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of such information; and protect against unauthorized access to or use of such information that may result in substantial harm or inconvenience to any consumer. The regulations shall take into account the person's size, scope and type of business, the amount of resources available to such person, the amount of stored data, and the need for security and confidentiality of both consumer and employee information. (b) The supervisor of records, with the advice and consent of the information technology division to the extent of its jurisdiction to set information technology standards under paragraph (d) of section 4A of chapter 7, shall establish rules or regulations designed to safeguard the personal information of residents of the commonwealth that is owned or licensed. Such rules or regulations shall be applicable to: (1) execut

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts M.G.L. c. 93H, §2, which mandates a comprehensive information security program for entities handling personal information of Massachusetts residents. The original text ensures security measures are at least as protective as those under applicable law. By contrast, the changed text allows the recipient to implement security measures they *deem* appropriate, potentially circumventing the specific requirements outlined in the law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state that the Department of Consumer Affairs and Business Regulation 'shall adopt regulations relative to any person that owns or licenses personal information about a resident of the commonwealth,' and that 'such regulations shall be designed to safeguard the personal information of residents of the commonwealth.' The modified text's discretionary language directly undermines this mandatory safeguarding, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Termination or expiration of this Agreement shall not relieve either party of its obligations hereunder to maintain in confidence and not to use Confidential Information received hereunder for a period of three (3) years from the date of receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Termination or expiration of this Agreement shall not relieve either party of its obligations hereunder to maintain in confidence and not to use Confidential Information received hereunder for a period of one (1) year from the date of termination thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period from three years from receipt to one year from termination significantly reduces the protection for confidential information. The effective period could be drastically shorter depending on when the information was disclosed, potentially undermining the purpose of the NDA and contradicting established expectations of reasonable protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93, § 42 (Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act) covers the misappropriation of trade secrets. While it doesn't prescribe a specific duration for confidentiality, it does state that an injunction can be issued to prevent the actual or threatened misappropriation of a trade secret. Changing the NDA from three years from the date of receipt to one year from termination, especially in sectors where trade secrets are vital, can be considered unreasonable and may not provide adequate protection for trade secrets, potentially undermining the protections envisioned by the law. A shorter term may be deemed insufficient, especially if the information retains its secret status and competitive advantage for a longer period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

(2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from three years from receipt to one year from termination, potentially undermining the protection of trade secrets, which contradicts the intent of M.G.L. c. 93, § 42. The law defines 'misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent, especially if the knowledge was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use. By shortening the confidentiality period, the changed text weakens this duty, potentially allowing for misappropriation if the confidential information is disclosed after only one year from termination, even if the information was received much earlier and retains its value as a trade secret. This undermines the protections envisioned by the law, making the modified contract legally questionable.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby e xpressly consent to the exclusive personal jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts for any dispute arising from or relating to this Agreement. If any term or condition of this Agreement shall be determined by any court of competent jurisdiction to be invalid or unenforceable to any extent, the remainder of this Agreement shall not be affected thereby, and each remaining term or condition hereof shall be valid and enforced to the fullest extent permitted by law. In the event such determination prevents the accomplishment of the purpose of this Agreement, the invalid provision shall be restated to conform with applicable law and to reflect as nearly as possible the original intention of the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereby consent to the personal jurisdiction of any court the Disclosing Party chooses for any dispute arising from or relating to this Agreement. If any term or condition of this Agreement shall be determined by any court to be invalid or unenforceable to any extent, the remainder of this Agreement shall be affected thereby, and each remaining term or condition hereof shall not be valid and enforced.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'exclusive' clause and giving the Disclosing Party the sole choice of jurisdiction creates an imbalance of power. Removing the severability clause makes the entire agreement vulnerable if any small part is deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 12(b)(2)
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRCP Rule 12(b)(2) itself allows a party to raise lack of personal jurisdiction as a defense, the original contract language provided for *exclusive* jurisdiction in Massachusetts. By modifying this to allow the Disclosing Party to choose *any* court and removing the severability clause, a challenge to jurisdiction based on minimum contacts (fundamental to due process) could invalidate the entire agreement. Even if the chosen court has jurisdiction, the clause can be seen as an overreach and could be challenged on the grounds of fairness and reasonableness, principles underlying the concept of personal jurisdiction and fairness inherent in the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 12. Defenses and Objections: When and How Presented; Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings; Consolidating Motions; Waiving Defenses; Pretrial Hearing | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 12. Defenses and Objections: When and How Presented; Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings; Consolidating Motions; Waiving Defenses; Pretrial Hearing (a) Time to Serve a Responsive Pleading. Unless another time is specified by a federal statute, the time for serving a responsive pleading is as follows: (1) In General. (A) A defendant must serve an answer: (i) within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint; or (ii) if it has timely waived service under Rule 4(d) , within 60 days after the request for a waiver was sent, or within 90 days after it was sent to the defendant outside any judicial district of the United States. (B) A party must serve an answer to a counterclaim or crossclaim within 21 days after being served with the pleading that states the counterclaim or crossclaim. (C) A party must serve a reply to an answer within 21 days after being served with an order to reply, unless the order specifies a different time. (2) United States and Its Agencies, Officers, or Employees Sued in an Official Capacity. The United States, a United States agency, or a United States officer or employee sued only in an official capacity must serve an answer to a complaint, counterclaim, or crossclaim within 60 days after service on the United States attorney. (3) United States Officers or Employees Sued in an Individual Capacity. A United States officer or employee sued in an individual capacity for an act or omission occurring in connection with duties performed on the United States’ behalf must serve an answer to a complaint, counterclaim, or crossclaim within 60 days after service on the officer or employee or service on the United States attorney, whichever is later. (4) Effect of a
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: mmittee in 1935 to solicit input from judges and practitioners and submit suggestions of rules. After solicitation and consideration, the Supreme Court approved the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure in 1938. One of the first major changes was the unity of courts of equity and law. Previously, as illustrated by the divergent treatment of procedural rules for federal courts serving as courts of equity in the Process Act of 1792 and for federal courts serving as courts of law in the Judiciary Act of 1789, the two were separate. Also, as a 1935 article in Yale Law Journal by Charles Clark and James Moore persuasively argued, many believed that any ultimate attempt to create a uniform system of procedural rules for federal courts would fail if equity and law were not united. The Advisory Committee and the Supreme Court would ultimately agree, and the new procedural rules unified the federal court’s heretofore separate roles as courts of equity and courts of law. Another major reform was the simplification of pleading requirements. Previously under the traditional formalistic common law method of pleading, as an article in Yale Law Journal by Charles Clark, History, Systems and Functions of Pleading , explains, a trial would not proceed until the parties reached a stage where one party affirmed and the other denied a single material point of contention to be ruled upon. This required extensive and technical pleading, which led to many of the miscarriages of justice that the ABA objected to in their push for reform. The reformed rules abandoned this system of pleading and allowed parties to hale their claims to court with simplified statements of their claims, and a streamlined process of objecting to and amending pleadings. Federal Rule of Civil Procedure Rule 8 lays out the general rules of pleading, and only requires a plaintiff to make a “short and plain statement of the claim showing that the pleader is entitled to relief. . . and a demand for the relief sought.” Rule 12 lays out when and how defenses and objections can be presented, and overall allows only certain objections to be raised past the pleading stage to ensure that the court can speedily address the dispute in contention. Commentators, jurists, and practitioners generally considered the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure to be a success. Charles Clark, a leading commentator on the reform, stated in his Yale Law Journal article, The Influence of Federal Procedural Reform , that “the universal chorus of approval is quite phenomenal.” Although the Rules have been amended many times in subsequent years, the core of original 1938 rules is still by-and-large intact. [Last updated in April of 2021 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS civil procedure Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies exclusive jurisdiction in Massachusetts, whereas the changed text allows the Disclosing Party to choose any court, potentially disregarding the original agreement's jurisdiction clause. Additionally, the original text includes a severability clause, ensuring the rest of the agreement remains valid if one part is unenforceable. The changed text removes this clause, making the entire agreement vulnerable if any term is deemed invalid. This directly contradicts standard legal practices that aim to preserve contracts as much as possible. Snippet 2 touches on how the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure aim to streamline the process, which is undermined by making the whole agreement unenforceable.
